Briefing: Middle School Student

Updated: 2026-04-05 Next meeting: 2026-04-07 View persona profile →

Brief: Amira (PERSONA-013)

Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-07

Since Last Meeting

No new inter-meeting evidence has come in since April 2.

At that meeting, the school board took no votes. Band and the percussion ed tech remain unresolved. Two things happened that are still in play going into April 7: the teachers’ union returned from Augusta with a potential $300,000 in state funding tied to specific student populations, and teacher Laura Stevens said on the record that the way students are kept in the dark when teachers leave might be deliberate — not just an oversight. Neither thread has been answered yet.

The April 7 meeting is a city council meeting, not a school board meeting. The school budget workshop at city council is scheduled for April 14. What happens April 7 sets the stage for that.

Open Questions

Amira is carrying these into the meeting:

  • Will band still exist at Memorial next year? The four music teachers and the percussion ed tech are still on the cut list. Nothing changed at April 2. Nobody said it was saved.
  • Did the $300,000 from Augusta actually come through? The union said “potential” — does anyone know if it’s confirmed, and will any of it go to music or related arts?
  • Why won’t anyone tell students when a teacher is leaving? A teacher said it might be on purpose. If that’s true, who decided that — and is it still happening right now?
  • Is there still time to change anything? The budget has failed twice. What actually happens next, and when does it get decided for real?
  • Why does the city get to give tax breaks to companies when the school is cutting teachers? There’s a tax abatement on tonight’s agenda. Amira’s heard this before with Texas Instruments. Who benefits from that, and what does it mean for the school budget?
  • After Kayler closes and the DEI director is cut, who speaks specifically for kids like her — BIPOC students, multilingual learners, kids with immigrant families — inside the district?
  • Is anyone in that building going to ask students what they think before the budget is finalized? The April 14 school workshop is next week. Nobody has asked.
  • The $100,000 for Project Home — is it enough? The families it’s meant to help are some of the same families whose kids go to school in South Portland. Is that crisis over, or still going?

Agenda Implications

Item G.2 — City Budget Presentation and Public Hearing (most important item for Amira)

This is the single item on tonight’s agenda that most directly affects what happens at Memorial next year. The city manager’s position paper lays out the full budget timeline:

  • April 7 (tonight): Public hearing on the city budget — the public can speak
  • April 14: Budget Workshop #1 — school budget is listed first
  • May 5: Public hearing and council vote on whether to send the school budget to voters
  • June 9: School budget referendum

Tonight is one of the last public comment opportunities before the council workshops the school budget on April 14. Anyone who wants to say something on record about band, the percussion ed tech, teacher positions, or anything else at Memorial has a real window tonight. The city council is hearing the school budget a week from now. What gets said tonight can still land before that.

Amira should listen for: whether any councilor asks specifically about the school budget during this presentation; whether the council frames the school as an education problem or a cost problem; and whether any councilor tells the public that April 14 is a place to show up and be heard.

Questions to prepare: When does public comment open for the April 14 school workshop? What’s the process — do people sign up in advance?

Item H.7 — Tax Abatement (Order, Abatement #26-02)

The agenda lists this as a city manager’s position paper. The specific details of the abatement aren’t visible in the agenda text, so the nature and size of the abatement are not yet known.

This matters to Amira because tax abatements reduce the revenue available for the city’s property tax base — the same base that funds 61% of South Portland’s school budget. Her cumulative understanding already includes the Texas Instruments TIF extension as one of several things competing for the same dollars as the school budget.

She should listen for: what entity is receiving the abatement, what the justification is, and how large the abatement is in dollar terms. She should not assume this is harmful — abatements are a standard tool, and some have legitimate economic development rationale — but the pattern of who receives tax relief and who absorbs cuts is worth tracking.

Question to prepare: What is the total value of this abatement, and how does it compare to the cuts being made to school programs?

Item H.1 — Postponed Order (from March 3 and March 19)

This item has been deferred at two consecutive council meetings. The agenda identifies it as ORDER #135-24/25 with an “Alternative Order” now being proposed. The specific subject of the order is not stated in the agenda text.

Two deferrals means the council has not been ready — or willing — to act on it twice. That pattern is worth noting without reading intent into it. Given the timing and the March dates of prior deferral, Amira should find out before the meeting what this order is about. If it touches housing, tenant protection, or any policy affecting immigrant families, it connects directly to the families whose children attend her school.

She should listen for: what the order actually covers when it’s introduced, and whether any councilor names the earlier deferrals as part of their comments.

Items D.10–D.16 — Board and Committee Vacancies

Several civic boards have open seats, including two that became vacant in January 2026 and three with anticipated vacancies at the end of April. The agenda doesn’t specify which boards these are for all positions.

This is lower priority for Amira than the budget items, but it’s worth knowing: the people appointed to these seats will be making civic decisions in the months ahead. If any of these are school-adjacent boards or community oversight committees, who gets appointed matters.

She doesn’t need to track this closely tonight, but she should notice: are these seats being filled quickly, and do appointees reflect the full range of South Portland’s community?

Items E.8–E.11 — Consent Agenda (Grants, Bids, Agreements)

Speed enforcement grant, a Maine Office of Community Affairs grant, pool deck flooring, and an agreement with SMCC. These are administrative items unlikely to directly affect Memorial or the school budget. The Maine Office of Community Affairs grant (E.9) is worth a quick glance — depending on its purpose, it could fund social services or community programs that affect families Amira knows — but the agenda doesn’t specify the subject of the grant.

Items H.2–H.4 and G.3–G.4 — E-Bike Ordinances and Marijuana Ordinances

Not relevant to Amira’s concerns tonight. She can let these pass without close attention.

Watch For

  • Whether any councilor names the school budget during the city budget public hearing — and whether they frame it as a cost problem (“we’re spending too much”) or an education problem (“we’re cutting things that matter to kids”). This framing has shifted back and forth across meetings and matters for what comes next at April 14.

  • Whether Councilor Scott or Councilor Matthews speak about the school budget — Scott has argued schools and ICE-affected families are the same problem; Matthews has argued they compete. Both may use tonight’s public comment period or budget discussion to advance their framing. Track whose logic councilors seem to follow.

  • Whether anyone at the public microphone tonight specifically mentions band, music teachers, the percussion ed tech, or any named position at Memorial — the April 2 school board meeting had speakers. City council has a public comment period too. Did any of those speakers shift to this venue?

  • The tax abatement’s scale — when the abatement amount is stated, compare it to numbers Amira already knows: $300K (potential Augusta funding), $100K (Project Home appropriation), $40K (pool deck flooring bid also on tonight’s agenda). This helps her place it in context.

  • Whether any councilor asks when the school board’s next budget meeting is — if the April 2 meeting produced no vote and the April 14 council workshop is approaching, the timeline is getting tight. A councilor asking this question signals they’re paying attention to the schedule; no one asking signals the opposite.

  • Whether the postponed H.1 order passes tonight or gets deferred a third time — the deferral pattern itself is informative. If it passes, find out what it was. If it’s deferred again, ask why.

  • How long the public comment period is and whether students or young people speak — Eva Morin and Lucy Hutzel walked to the microphone at a school board meeting without formal roles. City council public comment works the same way. If no young people speak tonight at a meeting that directly shapes the school budget calendar, that absence is part of the record.

  • Whether any councilor mentions the April 14 school workshop as an explicit public opportunity — if they do, that’s a signal that input before April 14 still matters; if they don’t, it suggests the workshop may be treated as internal deliberation rather than a public engagement point.

  • The board chair’s management of public comment — at school board meetings, DeAngelis stopped audience applause for student speakers multiple times. City council has its own procedural culture. Amira should notice whether respectful expressions of community support are permitted or managed away.

Last refreshed: 2026-04-05
